ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for pepper-and-spiced-salt fried prawns specifically; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific code exists for the deep-fried pepper-and-spiced-salt preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144007 identifies prawn as a substance; no procedure-specific code for this preparation exists.

preparation_method: seasoned with white pepper and spiced salt
garnished with chilli padi and curry leaves

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Prawns Fried with Pepper and Spiced Salt is approximately 300 g, representing a shared zi char plate of 4–6 whole prawns coated in toasted cereal and seasoned with pepper and spiced salt.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Prawn / Udang Goreng — IMR Malaysia MyFCD; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central (Shrimp, cooked) for macro cross-check
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: IMR MyFCD entry for fried prawn (udang goreng) used as the primary regional anchor. Values adjusted upward for fat and energy to account for the toasted cereal coating and butter used in this specific preparation, consistent with USDA data for breaded/battered fried shrimp (FDC ID 175180). Sodium elevated to reflect spiced salt seasoning. This is an adapted estimate; no exact match for this specific dish exists in any approved primary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood and cereal-coated fried dishes — analogous estimate based on battered/breaded fried shrimp and cereal coating GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawns Fried with Pepper and Spiced Salt. GI estimated at 48 based on: (1) plain fried/breaded shrimp has negligible glycemic impact from protein and fat; (2) toasted oat-based cereal coating contributes moderate carbohydrate with a GI analogous to oat-based products (GI ~50–55 per University of Sydney GI Database); (3) spiced salt and pepper contribute no glycemic load. Blended estimate of 48 is conservative and defensible. GL per serving = (48 × 10.5 × 300/100) / 100 = 15.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~620 mg per 100 g) from spiced salt seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from spiced salt; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,860 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from toasted cereal coating with low-to-moderate GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: The toasted cereal coating contributes approximately 10.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g with an estimated GI of 48 (low); a 300 g shared serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 15, which is moderate and should be factored into the total carbohydrate budget for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from butter and deep-frying oil, combined with high dietary cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally high in dietary cholesterol (~145 mg per 100 g) and the butter-and-oil frying method adds saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in small portions and infrequently, prioritising unsaturated fat sources in the remainder of their diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawns Fried with Pepper and Spiced Salt is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean zi char (煮炒) dish featuring whole prawns that are deep-fried and wok-tossed in a fragrant mixture of white pepper, spiced salt, garlic, butter, and curry leaves, then finished with a coating of toasted oat-based cereal for a distinctive crispy texture. The dish is garnished with sliced chilli padi for heat and fresh curry leaves for aroma. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, peppery, and aromatic, with a satisfying crunch from the cereal crust contrasting with the juicy, sweet prawn flesh. Nutritionally, the dish provides a meaningful protein contribution (~17.5 g per 100 g) from the prawns, moderate fat (~12 g per 100 g) from the frying medium and butter, and moderate carbohydrate (~10.5 g per 100 g) from the cereal coating. Sodium is elevated (~620 mg per 100 g) due to the spiced salt seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The estimated glycemic index is 48 (low), reflecting the oat-based cereal coating and the protein-and-fat-dominant macronutrient profile. Clinically, this dish is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its glycemic load, high concern for hypertension due to sodium, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to cholesterol and saturated fat content. It is commonly served as a shared plate in zi char restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia, typically priced as a premium seafood dish.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific dish (Prawns Fried with Pepper and Spiced Salt with toasted cereal coating) was found in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD fried prawn entries and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central breaded/battered fried shrimp data. GI is an analogous estimate based on oat-cereal coating GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted nature of the nutrient profile.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or USDA source entries used for this adapted estimate. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180, breaded shrimp) and IMR MyFCD fried prawn data; these are adapted estimates and not directly measured for this specific dish. 3. The description in the input JSON contained a likely OCR/translation error ('cuTy leaves' interpreted as 'curry leaves'). 4. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains butter and is served at a seafood restaurant where halal certification cannot be confirmed from available data. 5. GI estimated at 48 (low category, ≤55 threshold); glycemic_load_per_serving = (48 × 10.5 × 300/100) / 100 = 15.1. 6. All -1 sentinel values represent nutrients not reported in the primary adapted sources used. 7. Q8 fix (iteration 3): previous iteration contained mixed-script Hindi aliases (Tamil characters mixed with Devanagari) in aliases_by_language that were not consistently present in the flat aliases list. All Hindi aliases in aliases_by_language are now pure Devanagari script ('काली मिर्च और मसालेदार नमक के साथ तली हुई झींगा' and 'काली मिर्च झींगा') and both appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Malaysian/Singaporean cuisine (Chinese simplified, traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Adapted nutrient values from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FDC. Estimated GI at 48 using University of Sydney GI Database oat-cereal analogous data. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type values to allowed set only. Confirmed gi_category='low' for GI=48. Attempted to resolve Q8 consistency but introduced mixed-script Hindi aliases that caused the failure to persist.

Iteration 3: Replaced all mixed-script Hindi aliases (Tamil+Devanagari hybrid strings) with pure Devanagari Hindi aliases. Verified every string in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in the flat aliases array before finalising. Q8 consistency failure resolved.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
